item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahhh this one is good a medioccre writer who is litteraly his work. +1

I was like… "it's a mediocre playwright stuck in a mediocre play. Eh, neutral vote." up until the very last lines; having the O5 being a better playwright, with a nod to Shakespeare, made me laugh out loud and +1. Well done!

item.title: A Plus One
item.content:encoded:
Not merely about a good skip, it is
a work in the wake of the great Shakespeare.
I am partial to Shakespeare as he writes
in ways I wish that I could write today.
Fitting to critique a work as written.
No?

Mediocre cocept with flawless exicution and style.

The very last few lines were not very enjoyable for me but I can't not like this.

Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shakespeare mostly wrote theatrical dialog in iambic meter, but he wasn't as diligent about it as in his sonnets. Presumably because someone had to speak these lines and they needed to serve the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I love this! The standard SCP format actually lends itself beautifully to script format. I could see this article being acted out on stage to great effect.

I did notice as I was reading that a lot of Gloster's monologue doesn't really scan. However, he is supposed to be a mediocre playwright, so I think that actually works with the format.

Just as an unsolicited suggestion, I think it would be really fun if the dialogue with the researcher was written in broken-up rhyming couplets (not sure what the technical term for this style of dialogue is, but I've seen it a lot in Elizabethan plays — the thing where one speaker completes the other's couplet, comes across as very rapid-fire.) But prose works too, and makes the point that the researcher is just acting as he normally would.

I just loved reading this— thank you for writing it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I just don't understand the format, but I found the article hard to follow. With that aside, I have to say the idea was very original, and for that sake…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Tragedy of SCP-2763, A Modern Euclid Tale

I can't not like this.

And if anything, the reason this works is you didn't even try to follow standard format. Screw the format! you said. This is a format screw! Far too often, people write articles in rhyme or whatever and try to fit the containment procedures, the number, and the class up at the top where they belong. Bravely executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as format screws go, this one is on the tamer side of things, which works to its benefit in my opinion. Format screws always walk a fine line from degrading into hokey gimmicks.

The content of the article is interesting enough, and I do enjoy how much of a hack Golster is portrayed as.

+1. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview, as I understood it, is extremely interesting. How do you question someone who only exists in your writing?

This

You act and dress like a 16th century nobleman, but your vocabulary is decidedly modern, albeit with a faux-Elizabethan affectation, and your dental work can’t possibly have been done later than 1970

is only the case because the person 'conducting the interview' is writing it that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a neat idea! A man who poured his soul, literally, into his work. +1.

It does bug me a little that Gloster's monologue doesn't quite follow the "shortLONG shortLONG" pattern that Shakespearian writing does.
+ I tried to meter it out so it does follow the pattern, but that does make it harder to read.
- Dunno if this works at all.

So here again I sit, again you read.
As long as you will read, I'll sit; although
Beyond that time, I'll neither sit nor stand
For one who cannot be does neither. No,
My self cannot exist if it's not read.
My life: bound to this page that you now see.
I am not so much blood and bone and flesh
as I am ink, and paper, and story.

It was not always so. No, once was I
a mortal. Playwright, even! Though I lacked
the talent, I acknowledge. “So clumsy!
“Long-winded, vain! He does not have the knack!”
My work was ne'er desir'd. It would not last
more than a week! Dozens of plays I'd write
and not a word remembered longer than
a single fleeting pitiful fortnight.

One night I could not take it any more.
My razor, grabbed; my left wrist opened all;
And my closed right (the same that penned my plays)
Wrote “FIN” in great red letters on the wall.
I prayed to every god and spirit known.
I prayed to be forgotten; lost to time.
And if my name were to again be heard,
It be as author of a worthy rhyme.
Immortalized by my work, found sadly
After I left this world. But now I see
I should have spoken carefully; or not
Have said a word; or have written any.

So here I am. The cell I call my home
Sits empty, but for this: a lonely tome.
This is my life and times; the tragedy
Of me, and all I am, and ere shall be.

I do not breathe, yet you are sure that I
Do speak to you. And, save for as described
In stage direction, I can never move.
Yet you address me like I were alive
and stood in front of you. And if you try
to catalog me as a specimen
Or an experiment, then you will find
Poems and flow'ry prose flow from your pen

My words may bend and change from page to page,
But I am trapped here. All my world's a stage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, its simple in its idea, creepy enough on the idea of only existing when being read, yet he truly wishes to be able to die, to be forgotten. The format screw is really interesting me, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, yeah. I try to be direct but without telling folks to do, so that's why I miss examples sometimes.

+Not line-by-line, but Great Wall of Text!
-Great Wall of Text!

When I look at format screws, I'm looking a lot as to how the information is being presented and how much is changing from the format. Is it keeping enough consistency with SCP to still fit universe while still being distinct? Does the format change need to be made to present the same concept or information? Is the format change one that separates itself from other format screws on the site?
Let's compare this, as an example, to 2557. That is also, primarily, a word-based, written media format screw. While there are pictures, yes, if you removed them the format change would still stand on its own, and the article requires that format screw.

How does this hold up to an article like that, then, when examining the format screw alone? They're both writing-dependant media forms, are (for the most part) non-transformative, and both are off-tone for standard SCP writing. They're very similar, making my head jump to this - and others that follow similar format screws - instead of staying on your article. I'm not saying you directly tried to copy, but it does make me lose interest when I'm thinking of articles other than your own that tried similar concepts that, in my opinion, did it better.

While yours is different because of the aspect of it being a play, we're still talking about a format screw that can be accomplished purely at the idea and notepad-writing stage - and I'd argue the format screw in 2557 is similar in that regard.

Look at other format changes for examples as to what I'm talking about in regards to being "distinct" and "different". How many other of those format screws are ones that can persist on written paper? Of those, how many of them are trying to then present ideas similar to my upcoming points?

As far as the idea itself not being unique, this is actually a concept that can be found as early as the first 1000 series. 426 makes it impossible to write about it, think about it, or talk about it without being affected by it, 732 infects its own document and has infected other documents related to it and needs to be digitally quarantined. Outside of the 1000s is 1561, another object that talking about it forces you to talk differently about it.
As far as just the documentation changes occurring in and of themselves as a result of the anomalous effects, a close parallel can be drawn between 732 and this as they're both presumably sentient concepts that infect and change documents.
Heck, as long as we're talking about format screws and documentation changes, there's 2212.

Now 2763 does do something a bit different in this regard that I like; the idea is a bit meta ("Or minutes ago at your desk as you began transcribing this exchange? Or was I born here and now, at your screen, as you now read this documents") and has this sort of one-foot-in one-foot-out approach to Foundation language (mostly at the end, with the final O5 paragraph). I think both of these are strong parts of the article that carry it - and the self-awareness of the writing present is also something interesting.

As far as stakes go… Nothing about this seems to make it this crazy thing that the Foundation is feeling necessary to keep under wraps. Now, the Foundation does keep a *lot* of things locked up, and sometimes not for very good reasons. But more often than not, the story of the item itself has some sort of stake in it - some sort of risk, either on the side of the Foundation, or on the side of the world if the thing is not contained. With the examples listed here, 2557 might shatter humanity's perception of the universe and is something that in-and-of itself is keen to make the Foundation sweat (it's something on the Foundation files that they did not put there and can't seem to unput there). 426 has caused deaths. 732 could quite possibly set off nukes if it doesn't irreversibly corrupt large digital infrastructures first - like the Foundation itself. 15611 causes people to act with hostility.

Beyond this object merely being anomalous, wheres the risk and stake in the story? Obviously, it doesn't have to something major, world threatening, or mind bending, but here… Oh no, gosts. The Foundation is wanting to dedicate resources to keeping this thing locked tight, potentially lied to get their hands onto it, and wants the rest of the naive world to forget it exists - why is that? Why is that for this thing in particular? Or, at the very least, why does the Foundation *feel* like that's the case, even if it's incorrect? It's possible the answer is here, and I don't see it - but I'd argue that's still a problem with presentation.

I do like parts of this, as I mentioned above. I think that the tone and the meta-ness is something that works well for this piece, but ultimately I can't read this without being reminded of other articles, and pairing that with the lack of meaning to its containment is what hurts it enough for me to consider it a -1.

Footnotes
1. edit: said 1556, corrected

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had wondered the same thing, so I'm glad to see that explained. All in all, good work, I really like the format screw and it works well with the concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's very little substantial information to work with here. Could you provide me with examples?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While format screws can be interesting, I feel like this one in particular has been done before quite a bit and in better presentation - not in this exact method, but in the form of changing the written details while keeping to a similar medium and even telling.

On top of that, the idea itself isn't terribly interesting, and to be honest has kinda been done before.

Ultimately, as well, there's not much else to it… I think if you ever do a rewrite or make something similar, you should hold onto some of the ideas here (things like the tone and speaking/writing manner of the Scip), but change the presentation and add some stakes.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither he nor the researcher speak in verse, because they aren't mediocre cursed playwrights. His last lines are in verse because they are a direct, self-aware reference to the last lines of Shakespeare's Henry IV Part II.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is O5-8's dialog not consistent with the rest of the skip?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a painfully tragic story Sir Gloster has to tell. Even in the throes of death, his words betrayed his mediocrity. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I saw this was actually a main list I think I may have broken the upvote button.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will take the side of downvote this time. It ultimately feels like an attempt to write an SCP via a Shakespearian play and practically shred the SCP format in the process. By that technicality, I can hardly regard this an SCP article… and 'tis by that definition I make my choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to downvote but then I noticed it doesn't make things hard to read. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the cool and interesting format screw. I always find it interesting when people play with rituals and realize that they may have hecked up.

Also upvoted because I can read this like the space jam chorus. Come on and slam, and welcome to the jam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a format screw, yes - but one that doesn't feel contrived/tacked on, and uses the unorthodox format to its advantage instead of as a visual gimmick. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, it's written in iambic pentameter, no I didn't misspell Gloucester, yes those are Shakespeare references.

Special thanks to Thetataurus, minmin, JackIke, AidenEldritch, and the many other people in chat who looked this over for me.

Extra Special Thanks to Voct, who convinced me to write the whole damn thing in verse, and helped me out with some of that.
